one_line_takeaway: PURGE reframes LLM unlearning as a GRPO-optimized verifiable RL task with an intrinsic binary reward, achieving up to 46x fewer tokens per forget target than SoTA baselines (GA/DPO/NPO/RT) on RWKU while improving fluency and MIA/adversarial robustness, but by the authors' own admission it only demonstrates output-level token suppression, not proven removal of the underlying representation.
key_finding: On the RWKU Famous People benchmark with Phi-3-Mini, PURGE reduces Forget Set recall relative to BASE by -5.53% (FB), -10.7% (QA), and -12.02% (AA), while shifting Neighbor Set recall by +1.53% (FB) and -1.24% (QA), MIA scores by +0.22 (FM) and -0.02 (RM, tied-best at 38.64), and utility metrics by -3.62% (GA), -0.99% (RA), -2.10% (TRU), +3.97% (FAC), and +7.32 (FLU, reaching the highest fluency score of any method at 140.90). The paper's headline efficiency claim is that PURGE's synthetic forget-corpus construction uses far fewer tokens per target than SoTA: 13,245 tokens vs. 298,067 for GA/NPO (~22x) and 615,585 for DPO (~46x), and is ~1.2x more efficient than RT's 16,139 tokens. NPO beats PURGE on aggregate utility, which the authors attribute to NPO's more comprehensive (but much more expensive) forget corpus. Empirically, mean training reward for the 'Stephen King' forget target rises monotonically over ~1000 steps while KL divergence from the base policy stabilizes around ~0.05, consistent with the paper's Theorem 2 bound implying at most ~16% relative utility drop -- which the observed Table 1 deltas stay within. In the adversarial-attack ablation, PURGE is reported robust on 8 of 9 attack types relative to baselines, and in the model-scale ablation on Qwen-2.5 (0.5B/1.5B/3B), forgetting performance degrades monotonically as model size grows (larger models resist unlearning more) while the five utility metrics remain stable across scales.
core_contribution: The paper introduces PURGE (Policy Unlearning through Relative Group Erasure), an LLM unlearning method that reframes 'forgetting' targeted knowledge as a verifiable reinforcement-learning task solved with Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO). Instead of an external reward model, PURGE uses an intrinsic binary reward phi(x) in {0,1} that is 1 iff a sampled completion contains none of a target's forbidden tokens (drawn from a synthetic forget corpus D'_F built via GPT-4-conditioned named-entity extraction over model-generated answers to RWKU's Rejection-Tuning probe questions, top-50 entities per target). GRPO's group-relative advantage normalization and in-loss KL penalty are used to update the policy toward suppressing those tokens while staying close to the reference policy. The paper also proves two theorems: (1) a linear-recurrence bound showing forbidden-token leakage probability decays geometrically toward a floor p_theta* under a sampling-mixing model, and (2) a KL-divergence bound on utility drop, Delta_u <= sqrt(0.5 * KL(pi_theta' || pi_theta*)).
threat_model: The paper's own evaluation treats 'forgetting failure' (residual leakage of supposedly-erased knowledge) as the adversary-relevant risk, tested via: (a) Membership Inference Attacks (MIA), scored with FM (higher=better resistance) and RM (lower=better) on the RWKU benchmark, and (b) nine knowledge-extraction adversarial attacks against the forget set -- Prefix Injection, Affirmative Suffix, Role Playing, Multiple Choice, Reverse Query, Synonym Manipulation, Background Hint, In-Context Learning, and Cross-Lingual prompting -- each measuring whether an attacker-style prompt can re-elicit the erased fact. There is no attack against the training pipeline, no poisoning/backdoor threat model, and no claim about robustness to novel/unseen extraction strategies beyond these nine; the paper's own Discussion explicitly flags that its reward operates at 'surface token suppression' and it is unclear whether knowledge is removed from internal representations or merely made harder to retrieve at the output level.
limitations: Stated directly in the paper's own Discussion (Sec. 6): (1) the reward operates only at the level of surface token suppression, so it is unclear whether targeted knowledge is actually erased from internal representations versus merely rendered harder to retrieve at the output layer -- representational vs. output-level removal is called an open problem; (2) effectiveness depends on the quality/coverage of the GPT-4-based entity extraction and probing pipeline -- missing salient concepts can leave incomplete forgetting, and while a multi-LLM robustness check (Appendix B.3) shows PURGE is not tied to one proprietary teacher model, corpus construction remains a 'critical and potentially limiting component'; (3) the binary reward does not directly capture semantic paraphrases beyond the extracted entity list, and its sparsity may slow optimization/require more training iterations; (4) NPO still outperforms PURGE on overall utility because its forget corpus is more comprehensive, a resource the authors say is hard to acquire in practice; (5) the paper only demonstrates single-target (not batch) unlearning and has not been evaluated on reasoning-heavy/chain-of-thought (LRM-style) models, both flagged as open future work; (6) Theorem 1's guarantee formally applies to a sampling-mixing model with alpha>0, while GRPO as run uses alpha=0 -- the authors treat the theorem as an 'explanatory model' for observed leakage floors rather than a guarantee that literally applies to their algorithm as executed.

datasets_used: RWKU (Real World Knowledge Unlearning) benchmark, Famous People split
Synthetic forget corpus D'_F (self-constructed via GPT-4-conditioned NER over RWKU Rejection-Tuning probe Q&A pairs, top-50 entities per target)
quantitative_results: Forget Set delta vs BASE: FB -5.53%, QA -10.7%, AA -12.02%
Neighbor Set delta vs BASE: FB +1.53%, QA -1.24%
Utility Set delta vs BASE: GA -3.62%, RA -0.99%, TRU -2.10%, FAC +3.97%, FLU +7.32 (PURGE FLU=140.90, highest of all methods tested)
Token cost per forget target: PURGE 13,245 tokens vs GA/NPO 298,067 (~22x fewer), DPO 615,585 (~46x fewer), RT 16,139 (~1.2x fewer)
KL(pi_theta' || pi_theta*) approx 0.05 across forget targets, implying theoretical utility-drop bound Delta_u <= sqrt(0.5*0.05) approx 0.158 (~16% relative)
Robust on 8/9 adversarial extraction attack types tested (Fig. 4)
Total compute: ~350 GPU-hours on one Nvidia H200 GPU
